elements.sent-2411-001: Bonne and Censor-Hillel (ICALP 2019) initiated the study of distributed subgraph finding in dynamic networks of limited bandwidth.
elements.sent-2411-002: For the case where the target subgraph is a clique, they determined the tight bandwidth complexity bounds in nearly all settings.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, several open questions remain, and very little is known about finding subgraphs beyond cliques.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this work, we consider these questions and explore subgraphs beyond cliques.
elements.sent-2411-005: For finding cliques, we establish an $\Omega(\log \log n)$ bandwidth lower bound for one-round membership-detection under edge insertions only and an $\Omega(\log \log \log n)$ bandwidth lower bound for one-round detection under both edge insertions and node insertions.
elements.sent-2411-006: Moreover, we demonstrate new algorithms to show that our lower bounds are tight in bounded-degree networks when the target subgraph is a triangle.
elements.sent-2411-007: Prior to our work, no lower bounds were known for these problems.
elements.sent-2411-008: For finding subgraphs beyond cliques, we present a complete characterization of the bandwidth complexity of the membership-listing problem for every target subgraph, every number of rounds, and every type of topological change: node insertions, node deletions, edge insertions, and edge deletions.
elements.sent-2411-009: We also show partial characterizations for one-round membership-detection and listing.\yijun{remember to hide all comments before uploading to arxiv.}
elements.sent-2411-010: Detecting small subgraphs in distributed networks has recently attracted significant research interest, as many algorithms can be significantly improved if the underlying network does not contain certain small subgraphs.\yijun{While this is indeed one motivation, this is not really a main one, maybe try editing this later For instance, Pettie and Su showed distributed coloring algorithms for triangle-free graphs using fewer colors and rounds \cite{PETTIE2015263 .}}
elements.sent-2411-011: Similarly, Hirvonen, Rybicki, Schmid, and Suomela developed an efficient distributed algorithm to find large cuts in triangle-free graphs~\cite{hirvonen2014largecutslocalalgorithms .}
elements.sent-2411-012: Distributed subgraph finding plays an important role in understanding the bandwidth limitation in distributed networks: It is a classical problem where a straightforward $O(1)$-round solution exists with unlimited bandwidth, but becomes significantly more complex when bandwidth constraints are imposed.
elements.sent-2411-013: Previous works on distributed subgraph findings mostly assumed a model in which the underlying network is static.
elements.sent-2411-014: However, distributed systems in real life may undergo topological changes over time: A node might crash, and a new connection might be formed between two existing nodes.
elements.sent-2411-015: To address this gap, Bonne and Censor-Hillel~\cite{bonne2019distributed initiated the study of distributed subgraph finding in dynamic networks to better capture the real-world behavior of networks of {limited bandwidth .}}
elements.sent-2411-016: Later, Liu~\cite{liu2022noteimprovedresultsround extended this study to dynamic graphs with batched updates and resolved an open question of Bonne and Censor-Hillel~\cite[Open question 4]{bonne2019distributed .}}
elements.sent-2411-017: In this paper, we build upon their works~\cite{bonne2019distributed,liu2022noteimprovedresultsround to consider the remaining open questions and explore other target subgraphs beyond cliques.}
elements.sent-2411-018: We now formally describe the models considered in this paper, which were introduced by Bonne and Censor-Hillel~\cite{bonne2019distributed .}
elements.sent-2411-019: A dynamic network $\mathcal{G}$ is a sequence of graphs $\mathcal{G =\{G^0, G^1, \ldots \}$.}
elements.sent-2411-020: The superscript notation should not be confused with graph powers.
elements.sent-2411-021: The initial graph $G^0$ represents the state of the network at some starting point.
elements.sent-2411-022: For each $i > 1$, the graph $G^i$ is either identical to its preceding graph $G^{i-1}$ or obtained from $G^{i-1}$ by a single topological change.
elements.sent-2411-023: Each node $u$ in the network is equipped with a unique identifier $\ID(u)$, and it knows the list of identifiers of all its neighbors.
elements.sent-2411-025: In each round of communication, each node can send to each of its neighbors a message of $B$ bits, where $B$ denotes the bandwidth of the network.
elements.sent-2411-026: We assume that each node initially knows the entire topology of the initial graph $G^0$.
elements.sent-2411-027: In particular, the set of all identifiers is global knowledge, so we may assume that the range of the identifiers is exactly $[n]$, where $n$ is the number of nodes in the network.
elements.sent-2411-028: We consider four types of topological changes: node insertions, node deletions, edge insertions, and edge deletions.

elements.sent-2411-044: We consider the four types of distributed subgraph finding problems introduced by Bonne and Censor-Hillel~\cite{bonne2019distributed .}
elements.sent-2411-045: For the membership-listing ($\MemList(H)$) problem, each node $v$ lists all the copies of $H$ containing $v$.
elements.sent-2411-046: In other words, for each copy of $H$ and each node $u$ in $H$, node $u$ lists $H$.
elements.sent-2411-047: For the membership-detection ($\MemDetect(H)$) problem,~ each node $v$ decides whether $v$ belongs to at least one copy of $H$.
elements.sent-2411-048: For the listing ($\List(H)$) problem, every appearance of $H$ is listed by at least one node in the network.
elements.sent-2411-049: In other words, for each copy of $H$, there exists some node $u$ that lists $H$.
elements.sent-2411-050: For the detection ($\Detect(H)$) problem, the existence of any $H$ must be detected by at least one node.
elements.sent-2411-051: Specifically, if the network does not contain $H$ as a subgraph, then all nodes must output $\No$.
elements.sent-2411-052: Otherwise, at least one node must output $\Yes$.
elements.sent-2411-053: For both membership-detection and detection, the output of each node is binary ($\Yes/\No$) only, with no requirement to report the actual target subgraph.
elements.sent-2411-054: For both membership-listing and listing, each node outputs a list of the target subgraphs, using the node identifiers in the network.
elements.sent-2411-055: For the listing problem, the node $u$ responsible for listing $H$ is not required to be in $H$, and it is allowed that each copy of $H$ is listed by multiple nodes.
elements.sent-2411-056: The $r$-round bandwidth complexity of a problem is defined as the minimum bandwidth $B$ for which there exists an $r$-round algorithm solving that problem with bandwidth $B$.
elements.sent-2411-057: Fix a target subgraph $H$, a round number $r$, and some type of topological changes.

elements.sent-2411-627: If H is a subgraph with $\diam(H)=2$ and $\rad(H)=1$, there is an $1$-round, $O(1)$-bandwidth algorithm for $\MemDetect(H)$ under node deletions only.
elements.sent-2411-628: Since $\rad(H)=1$, there is a node in any copy of $H$ that is adjacent to every node in the same copy of $H$.
elements.sent-2411-629: We pick one such node for each copy and call this node the central node and other nodes the fringe nodes.
elements.sent-2411-630: Initially, all nodes list every copy of $H$ they belong to, and output \Yes{ if the number is at least $1$.}
elements.sent-2411-631: In subsequent rounds, the central node $u$ in every copy will inform each fringe node $v$ once it detects that all copies of $H$ with $u$ as the central node and $v$ as a fringe node has been destroyed (due to the deletion of certain fringe node in a copy of $H$).
elements.sent-2411-632: When a central node $u$ of a copy is removed, the fringe nodes will remove all the copy of $H$ such that $u$ is the central node in that copy.
elements.sent-2411-633: Each node will output \Yes{ if and only if it is still in some copy of $H$, either as a central node or as a fringe node.}
elements.sent-2411-634: The bandwidth is $O(1)$, since we only send $1$-bit messages from the central node.

elements.sent-2411-641: Since $H$ is a non-trivial complete multipartite graph, for any node $v\in V(H)$, every node $u\in V(H)$ is either a neighbor of $v$, or is adjacent to every node in $N_H(v)$.
elements.sent-2411-642: For nodes of the latter case, we call them the independent nodes of $v$ since they are the nodes that are in the same independent set as $v$ in $H$.
elements.sent-2411-643: The deletion of neighbors of $v$ can be detected by $v$ immediately, while the deletion of any independent node of $v$ can be detected by every neighbor of $v$.
elements.sent-2411-644: Hence, our idea is to use the neighbors of $v$ to help $v$ keep track of its independent nodes.
elements.sent-2411-645: But in the network $G$, each node $w$ may be part of more than 1 copy of $H$, say $H_i$ and $H_j$, and be in different partitions of $H_i$ and $H_j$.
elements.sent-2411-646: Hence, every node $w$ needs to ``act'' as each partition in $H$, and count the number of nodes that are adjacent to every node in $S\subseteq N_G(w)$, where $S$ edge-contains a copy of the other parts of $H$.
elements.sent-2411-647: This count can be efficiently maintained by having nodes notify their remaining neighbors whenever they detect a neighbor deletion.

elements.algorithm-2411-001: \SetKwHangingKw{Output}{Output} \SetKwHangingKw{Require}{Require} \SetKw{Send}{Send} \SetKw{Receive}{Receive} \SetKwBlock{Init}{Initialize}{end} \SetKwBlock{Rounds}{{ In Round $r$} }{{ end}} \caption{$\MemDetect(K_C)$ under node deletions for each node $v$}\label{alg:K_C-ND} \Require{$K_C = K_{c_1, \dots, c_k}$, $c_i \geq 2$ for all $i\in [k]$ and $\sum_{i\in [k]}c_i = |V(K_C)| = \kappa$ } \Init{ \For{$i\in [k]$}{ $M_i \gets \{S: S \subseteq N^0(v)$, $ |S|=\kappa - c_i$, and $K_C^i \subseteq G^0[S]\}$\; \tcp*[f]{\textnormal{ $M_i$ maintains a collection of subsets of $v$'s neighbors that form $K_C^i$.}} \For{$S \subseteq N^0(v)$}{ $C_v^0(S;K_C^i)\gets \Count_v^0(S;K_C^i)$; } } } \Rounds{ \If( \tcp*[f]{\textnormal{ $N^r(v)=N^{r-1}(v)\setminus\{u\}$}} ){$u \in N^{r-1}(v)$ is deleted}{ \For{$i \in [k]$}{ \For{$S \in M_i$ such that $u\in S$}{ $M_i \gets M_i \backslash \{S\}$;\label{line:memdetect-nd-update-mi} } } \Send{} \Del{} to each node in $N^r(v)$ \tcp*{\textnormal{ \Del{} is a $1$-bit message}} } \Receive{} messages (if any) from $N^r(v)$\; \For{$S\subseteq N^r(v)$}{ \For{$i\in [k]$}{ \eIf{$S \in M_i$ and $v$ receives \Del{} from all nodes in $S$}{ $C_v^r(S;K_C^i) \gets C_v^{r-1}(S;K_C^i)-1$\; \label{line:memdetect-nd-update-K_C} }{ $C_v^r(S;K_C^i) \gets C_v^{r-1}(S;K_C^i)$\; \label{line:memdetect-nd-same-K_C} } } } \eIf( ){$C_v^r(S;K_C^i) \geq c_i-1$ for some $i\in [k], S \subseteq N^r(v)$}{ \tcp*[f]{\textnormal{ $v$ and other $c_i-1$ nodes form a subset of size $c_i$, forming a $K_C$ together with $S$}} \Output{\Yes{}} }{ \Output{\No{}} } }
